title: Applied Python Projects
subtitle: Build cleaner Python projects with classes, testing, debugging, and capstone structure
intendedFor: Learners who know Python fundamentals and want to build more complete beginner projects using classes, testing, debugging, and readable project structure.

courseOverview.recommendedSequence: Complete modules in order. This course moves from object-oriented programming to testing and then to a complete capstone project.
courseOverview.summary: This course helps learners build polished beginner Python projects using classes, objects, tests, debugging techniques, style habits, and capstone planning.

moduleSummary.title: Object-Oriented Programming
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Introduce classes and objects as a way to model related state and behavior.
moduleSummary.outputFocus: Build a simple class-based gradebook, game character, or bank account model.

moduleSummary.title: Testing, Debugging, and Style
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Help learners verify code behavior, debug systematically, and write readable Python.
moduleSummary.outputFocus: Take a buggy utility program, add tests, fix the bugs, and improve readability.

moduleSummary.title: Capstone Automation Project
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Guide learners through a complete beginner Python project that combines core skills.
moduleSummary.outputFocus: Complete a capstone such as a task tracker, expense summarizer, text analyzer, or quiz game.

authoringGuidance: This course must always use profileId python and subjectSlug applied-python-projects-v2.
This course should use the browser-python-files-runner workspace across the full course.
Because learners already know imports and modules here, exercises may use starter files, helper modules, folders, and filesystem paths when that supports the lesson goal.
Prefer files-panel language such as main.py, helpers.py, src/, data/, and tests/ over single-file-only framing.
Do not use SQL datasets, SQL tables, sql_query recipes, datasetId, fixedSqlDialect, resultShape, or checkSql in Python lessons.
Python code_input exercises should prefer recipeType fixed_tests and include explicit stdin/stdout tests or function/class behavior checks.
Use semanticChecks only when validating classes, methods, attributes, or return values.
Module 8 class topics should prefer recipeType semantic for class/object/method behavior instead of fixed_tests stdout wrappers.

For classes-and-instances, attributes-and-init, and methods-and-self, use semantic checks such as defines_class, constructible, instance_attributes, and method_returns when the learner is returning values from methods.
Do not expose ast.literal_eval, _parse_arg, or visible stdin-parsing harness code in class or method exercises.
For method_returns semantic checks, always include expected.
Keep examples focused on classes, objects, testing, debugging, style, refactoring, and capstone projects.

assessmentAndDelivery.suggestedBeginnerRhythm: Two or three short lessons per week, with one applied project after each module.
toolingSuggestions: Browser runner or local VS Code
Simple test runner for function-based and class-based exercises
assessmentAndDelivery.closingNote: Learners finish with a polished beginner Python project and a stronger foundation for automation, data science, web backends, scripting, or algorithms.

notes: Use semantic code_input exercises for class definition, constructor, attribute, and method-return tasks in this module.
Avoid fixed_tests stdout wrappers when the learner is defining a class or returning values from instance methods.
Prefer semantic checks like defines_class, constructible, instance_attributes, and method_returns for module 8 class topics.
Prefer semantic checks for class construction and simple method behavior.
Do not generate fixed_tests that expect stdout from a class-return task.
Treat create-an-instance-and-call-a-method tasks as semantic class behavior tasks, not print-output tasks.
Use semantic checks for constructor arguments and instance attributes.
Do not use stdin/stdout wrappers for __init__ attribute tasks.
Use semantic method_returns checks when learners define instance methods that return values.
Do not expose visible parser harness code or ast.literal_eval wrappers.

modules.title: Object-Oriented Programming
modules.purpose: Introduce classes and objects as a way to model related state and behavior.
modules.moduleProject: Build a simple class-based gradebook, game character, or bank account model.

topics.title: Classes and Instances
topics.summary: Define classes and create individual objects.
learningGoals: Create simple Python classes from real examples.
Create separate objects from the same class.

topics.title: Attributes and __init__
topics.summary: Initialize object state with constructor parameters.
learningGoals: Create small Python classes that store state with **init**.
Pass starting data into a new object.

topics.title: Methods and self
topics.summary: Add behavior to classes with instance methods.
learningGoals: Use methods to make objects perform useful actions.
Use `self` to read and update object state.

topics.title: Encapsulation and Responsibility
topics.summary: Keep related data and behavior together in a class.
learningGoals: Keep related data and behavior inside one class.
Give each class one clear responsibility.

topics.title: Lists of Objects
topics.summary: Manage multiple instances and summarize their data.
learningGoals: Store multiple objects in a list and loop through them.
Use object data to build small summaries.

topics.title: Class Debugging Patterns
topics.summary: Fix common attribute, method, and constructor mistakes.
learningGoals: Find and fix common class mistakes.
Trace constructor, attribute, and method bugs.

topics.title: Module 8 Class Model Project
topics.summary: Build a class-based project in small steps so each new method and object behavior grows from the previous working version.
learningGoals: Extend one class-based project in small working steps.
Use objects to model state and behavior in a small tool.

modules.title: Testing, Debugging, and Style
modules.purpose: Help learners verify code behavior, debug systematically, and write readable Python.
modules.moduleProject: Take a buggy utility program, add tests, fix the bugs, and improve readability.

topics.title: Writing Test Cases
topics.summary: Design examples that prove a function works.
learningGoals: Write simple tests for normal cases and edge cases.
Choose examples that prove a function works.

topics.title: Assertions and Expected Results
topics.summary: Use assertions to compare actual and expected outputs.
learningGoals: Use `assert` to compare actual and expected results.
Read a failing assertion to spot what broke.

topics.title: Testing Edge Cases
topics.summary: Check empty inputs, boundaries, and unusual values.
learningGoals: Test empty inputs, boundary values, and unusual cases.
Use edge cases to catch hidden bugs.

topics.title: Debugging With Print and Trace
topics.summary: Use temporary output and tracing to find bugs.
learningGoals: Use print tracing to follow code step by step.
Narrow a bug down to the exact failing step.

topics.title: Readable Python Style
topics.summary: Use naming, spacing, and small functions to make code easier to understand.
learningGoals: Use clear names and spacing to make code easier to read.
Break long logic into small readable helpers.

topics.title: Refactoring Safely
topics.summary: Improve code structure while keeping behavior the same.
learningGoals: Refactor small pieces without changing behavior.
Use tests to confirm a refactor stayed correct.

topics.title: Module 9 Debug and Polish Project
topics.summary: Improve a buggy utility in focused steps so tests, fixes, and readability improvements build on the previous working version.
learningGoals: Use tests, tracing, and cleanup to improve one program step by step.
Keep behavior stable while you fix bugs and readability.

modules.title: Capstone Automation Project
modules.purpose: Guide learners through a complete beginner Python project that combines core skills.
modules.moduleProject: Complete a capstone such as a task tracker, expense summarizer, text analyzer, or quiz game.

topics.title: Choosing a Capstone Scope
topics.summary: Pick a project that is useful, small enough, and testable.
learningGoals: Choose a project idea that is small, useful, and testable.
Cut a big idea down to a realistic first version.

topics.title: Breaking Projects Into Functions
topics.summary: Plan functions, inputs, outputs, and data flow before coding.
learningGoals: Split a small automation project into helper functions.
Plan what each function receives and returns.

topics.title: Data Model for a Small App
topics.summary: Choose lists, dictionaries, files, or classes to represent project data.
learningGoals: Choose simple data structures for a small app.
Match your data model to the features you want to build.

topics.title: Implementing the Core Loop
topics.summary: Build the main interaction loop or workflow for the project.
learningGoals: Build the main loop or workflow for a small project.
Handle repeated user choices in a clear order.

topics.title: Testing the Capstone
topics.summary: Test the most important functions and realistic user paths.
learningGoals: Test the most important paths in your capstone.
Find and fix bugs across several working pieces.

topics.title: Presenting and Documenting the Project
topics.summary: Write a short README-style explanation and show how to run the project.
learningGoals: Explain what your project does and how to run it.
Write short notes that help another learner use the project.

topics.title: Capstone Automation Project
topics.summary: Complete a final automation project that carries a working solution through implementation, testing, and presentation.
learningGoals: Grow one final Python project through several focused changes.
Combine planning, building, testing, and polish in one workflow.
Finish with a complete beginner portfolio project.
